content.text:
*"I have set my rainbow in the clouds, and it will be 
the sign of the covenant between me and the earth."*{Genesis 9:13}{The four color theorem: every planar map can be colored with at most four colors.}{Kenneth Appel}

**Graph coloring** assigns colors to vertices 
so that adjacent vertices have different colors.

content.title: Proper Coloring
content.content:
A **proper $k$-coloring** of graph $G$ assigns 
one of $k$ colors to each vertex so that no two adjacent vertices share 
the same color.

The **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ is the 
minimum $k$ for which $G$ has a proper $k$-coloring.

content.title: Chromatic numbers
content.problem:
- $\chi(K_n) = n$ (all vertices adjacent, all need different colors)

- $\chi(C_n) = 2$ if $n$ even, 3 if $n$ odd

- $\chi(K_{m,n}) = 2$ (bipartite graphs are 2-colorable)

content.title: Exam scheduling
content.problem:
Vertices = exams; edges connect exams with common students. A proper 
coloring assigns time slots so no student has two exams simultaneously.

content.title: Map coloring
content.problem:
Vertices = countries; edges connect neighboring countries. A proper 
coloring assigns colors so neighboring countries differ.

content.title: Four Color Theorem
content.content: Every planar graph can be properly colored with at most 4 colors.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
A **planar graph** can be drawn in the plane 
with no edge crossings.

The Four Color Theorem was proved in 1976 by Appel and Haken using 
computers—the first major theorem proved with computational assistance.

content.title: The Four Color Problem
content.content:
The Four Color Conjecture was posed in 1852 and resisted proof for over 
a century. The computer-assisted proof raised philosophical questions: 
Can a proof be trusted if no human can verify every case? Most 
mathematicians accept the proof, though some wish for a "human-readable" 
alternative.

content.title: Brooks' Theorem
content.content:
If $G$ is connected and not a complete graph or odd cycle, then 
$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$.
content.label: Theorem

marginNotes.text:
The foundations of the city walls 
were decorated with every kind of precious stone.
